text: The surgical setup includes one camera and two robotic instruments positioned through the ports for the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection on the medial pleura located on the aortic side within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon utilizes a bipolar Maryland forceps and a monopolar cautery device during the thoracic dissection phase of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon avoids grasping the pleura during the thoracic dissection, maintaining careful manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon holds the pleura and applies retraction to facilitate dissection in the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon performs fine dissection on the medial pleura using bipolar cautery, avoiding blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon separates the inferior pulmonary ligament, using precise dissection techniques to detach it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully removes lymph nodes along with the esophageal specimen, ensuring complete excision of the nodes during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters significant bleeding while removing the subcranial lymph node during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation technology to visualize and coagulate smaller vessels in the subcranial area, facilitating the removal of the specimen en bloc with the esophagus.
The surgeon displays the level eight and nine lymph nodes, which have been excised along with the esophageal specimen.
